texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper analysed the performance of a membrane system over key design/operation parameters. A computation methodology is developed to solve the model of hollow fibre membrane systems for multicomponent gas feeds. The model represented by a nonlinear differential algebraic equation system is solved via a combination of backward differentiation and Gauss-Seidel methods. Natural gas sweetening problem is investigated as a case study. Model parametric analyses of variables, namely feed gas quality, pressure, area, selectivity and permeance, resulted in better understanding of operating and design optima. Particularly, high selectivities and/or permeabilities are shown not to be necessary targets for optimal operation. Rather, a medium selectivity (<60 in the given example) combined with medium permeance ( ∼ 300-500 × 10 -10 mol/s m 2 Pa in the given case study) is more advantageous. This model-based membrane systems engineering approach is proposed for the synthesis of efficient and cost-effective multi-stage membrane networks.

texts.orig: Membrane separation is growing as a significant unit operation for gas separation. The main advantages of membrane separation over other technologies include compactness, modularity, ease of skid-mounted installation, ability to be applied in remote areas such as offshore, flexibility in operation and maintenance and in most cases lower capital cost as well as lower energy consumption (Seader and Henley, 2006). Bernardo et al. (2009) have given a review of the state-ofthe-art developments in membrane materials for industrial applications in gas separation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Accepted achieve required purities at minimum capital and operational expenditures (CAPEX and OPEX). Modelling and optimisation are essential in MSE work which are nowadays more facilitated by faster computers and enhanced algorithms. Membranes require high pressures in order to facilitate mass transfer. With notable increase in energy prices within the last decade, the importance of MSE has notably increased in order to minimise the costs and thus enhance the overall performance of membranes especially in the conditions that there are competing alternatives for membranes. This paper focuses on MSE, and discusses the complementary nature of MMD and MSE, particularly emphasising that optimal material designs are a strong function of systems design.

texts.orig: Stern et al. (1965) presented a general algorithm for multicomponent gas mixtures. It was a trial-and-error method in perfect-mixing flow condition. Having a predefined stagecut, a permeate component fraction was assumed from where membrane area and then other components' fractions in the permeate were calculated. If the sum of all calculated fractions was not unity, the same procedure was repeated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Though there were other efforts (Pan and Habgood, 1978a,b; Sengupta and Sirkar, 1984) that tackled this problem, it was Shindo et al. (1985) who presented a complete calculation method for multicomponent gas mixtures at five different flow patterns (one-side-mixing, perfect mixing, cross flow, countercurrent and cocurrent). The study was based on flat membranes with the assumption of negligible pressure drop on both sides of the membrane. It concluded that countercurrent flow could result in higher permeate purity. In another study, Li et al. (1990), possibly without knowing the results by Shindo et al. (1985), modelled multicomponent gas flow in five different flow patterns over flat membranes. They also reported the countercurrent flow to be the most efficient whilst the perfect-mixing was the least. Pan (1986) developing his previous models (Pan and Habgood, 1974, 1978a,b; Pan, 1983) presented a model of multicomponent permeation with high-flux, asymmetric hollow-fibre membranes. The model considered the permeate pressure variation inside the fibre. However, in the validation stage, negligible permeate pressure drop was assumed, perhaps due to computational limitations of that time. This assumption simplified the set of equations including ordinary differential equations (ODEs) allowing an analytical solution. Pan, thence, proposed an equation for obtaining membrane area of multicomponent gas mixtures.

texts.orig: A thorough review of efforts done for gas membrane modelling till 1992 could be found in Kovvali et al. (1992). Later, Kovvali et al. (1994) admitting the solution complicacy of Pan's set of nonlinear equations, proposed a simplified solution methodology by assuming a linear relationship between the permeate and feed stream compositions. They had previously used this method for binary mixtures (Krovvidi et al., 1992). This way they could reduce the computational efforts whilst having acceptable accuracy.

texts.orig: In a different type of modelling, Coker et al. (1998) knowing the numerical solution limitations of the set of nonlinear ODEs generated from multicomponent hollow fibre modelling (such as the one by Pan, 1986) proposed an algebraic stagewise method to solve the problem. They divided the length of hollow fibre into a series of S stages in the axial direction and mass balances were enforced in each section. They developed a model for cocurrent, countercurrent, and cross flow contacting patterns considering permeate sweep, pressuredependent permeability coefficients, and bore side pressure gradients.

texts.orig: In this study we use Pan's model and propose a general finite difference method coupled with Gauss-Seidel algorithm for the solution of the non-linear membrane differential algebraic equations (DAEs). The paper presents the proposed solution algorithm followed by simulation results and analyses on the impact of key parameters on the membrane separation. As a case-study, we use natural gas sweetening problem and perform parametric analysis over pressure, area, fibre size, selectivity, permeability, etc. Critical results and discussion on the interactive impacts of these parameters are then given and some optimality regions are identified being essential for optimal membrane system design.

The paper presents the proposed solution algorithm followed by simulation results and analyses on the impact of key parameters on the membrane separation. As a case-study, we use natural gas sweetening problem and perform parametric analysis over pressure, area, fibre size, selectivity, permeability, etc. Critical results and discussion on the interactive impacts of these parameters are then given and some optimality regions are identified being essential for optimal membrane system design.

texts.orig: Membranes, from a permselectivity aspect, are categorised into three groups; macroporous, microporous and nonporous (dense), where gas membranes are of the latter type (Pabby et al., 2008). Macroporous membranes exhibit very high porosity and are only used when the process is pressurised which requires these membranes to have strong supports. Microporous membranes have pore sizes above 10 ˚ A, making them unsuitable for gas separation. However, they are usually used as a support for the low-permeable dense membranes. In order to improve the separation performance of dense membranes, permeance is increased by using very thin dense layer ( l 2) whilst a microporous layer is used ( l 3) to support the membrane structure. Dense membranes do not have voids due to the thermodynamic-equilibrium formation, whilst glassy membranes have voids (Paul and Yampol'skii, 1994).

texts.orig: Besides thermodynamic and mass transfer properties, membrane module design and flow patterns also affect membrane separation (Geankoplis, 1993). There are four main types of membrane designs, i.e. flat (plate-and-frame), tubular, spiralwound and hollow-fibre. The latter is receiving increasing attention (especially for gas separation) due to its high packing density as a result of higher area per volume (Seader and Henley, 2006). Five flow patterns have also been studied, i.e. one-side-mixing, perfect mixing, cross flow, cocurrent and countercurrent flow (Geankoplis, 1993; Shindo et al., 1985). For hollow fibre systems the practical options are cocurrent and countercurrent flows (Fig. 2).

For hollow fibre systems the practical options are cocurrent and countercurrent flows (Fig. 2).

texts.orig: Considering the gas flow has C components ( i = 1, 2, . . . , C ), we seek the concentration profile of both feed ( xi ) and permeate ( yi ) over the hollow fibre length. The profiles of feed gas flowrate and permeate pressure are also required to be calculated. The profile of feed gas pressure is not required to be calculated as we assumed it not to change over the fibre length. The calculations of permeate flowrate ( Fp ) and bulk concentrations ( yp i ) are also redundant as by having feed gas flowrate profile, they could be easily obtained using mass balance equations (25) and (26) for concurrent and countercurrent flows, respectively. Therefore, we have 2 C + 2 unknowns. The same number of equations is required to solve the problem. Eqs. (13), (14), (19) and (21) are candidate equations. These equations form a system of nonlinear differential-algebraic equation (NDAE). To solve this problem we convert the ODEs into algebraic equation using backward finite differential equations over j segments ( j = 1, 2, . . . , J ) of the fibre.

The calculations of permeate flowrate ( Fp ) and bulk concentrations ( yp i ) are also redundant as by having feed gas flowrate profile, they could be easily obtained using mass balance equations (25) and (26) for concurrent and countercurrent flows, respectively. Therefore, we have 2 C + 2 unknowns. The same number of equations is required to solve the problem. Eqs. (13), (14), (19) and (21) are candidate equations. These equations form a system of nonlinear differential-algebraic equation (NDAE). To solve this problem we convert the ODEs into algebraic equation using backward finite differential equations over j segments ( j = 1, 2, . . . , J ) of the fibre.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Having converted the ODEs into algebraic equations, we need to solve Eq. (19) along with newly developed algebraic equations simultaneously. We use Gauss-Seidel algorithms to solve these equations. In this method, each variable is first defined with an initial guess wherefrom the new values are subsequently obtained from the equations. If differences between old and new values are less than a given tolerance the problem for stage j is solved and solution algorithm proceeds with the next segment. If the values are more than the tolerance, the current values are substituted in old values and the procedure is repeated. The solution procedure is presented in Figs. 4 and 5 for countercurrent and concurrent flow, respectively. The only difference in the solution algorithm for these flow patterns is the initial values. For instance, for countercurrent flow, the retentate flowrate ( Fr or Ff j = J ) is unknown which must be guessed. For concurrent flow, however, the permeate pressure at j = 0 ( Pp 0 ) is not available and instead we have Pp J . Therefore, Pp 0 must be guessed. The algorithm was implemented and solved using MATLAB R2009a (Mathworks, USA) with a desktop computer featuring 2-quad CPU of 2.83 GHz and 4 GB of RAM. The execution time was variable for different runs and was dependent of the given parameters.

texts.orig: We use the natural gas sweetening problem to illustrate the utility of the model. Natural gas sweetening is an economically significant industrial problem. The existence of sour gases like CO2 in crude natural gas coming from wells has two major disadvantages; (1) increases corrosion and (2) reduces natural gas calorific value and thus increases OPEX. It is general consensus that sales gas should have CO2 less than 2% (Bhide and Stern, 1993). For the purpose of this modelling exercise, we discuss a natural gas containing four components, methane, ethane, propane and CO2 at different operating conditions. The selectivity values of 36 (CO2 over methane), 0.275 (ethane over methane) and 0.0536 (propane over methane) along with CO2 permeance of 134 × 10 -10 mol/s m 2 Pa have been used in our analyses (Pan, 1986).

texts.orig: The permeate concentration profile is usually of most interest and a benchmark for membrane evaluation, selection of flow pattern and other design factors. Fig. 9 illustrates the CO2 concentration (molar fraction) in permeate for cocurrent and countercurrent flows. For the cocurrent pattern, once the feed gas enters the membrane, due to the concentration of CO2 in the feed being at its maximum, the permeate concentration of CO2 at the beginning of membrane (blocked end) becomes the highest (84.97%). However, as the feed gas flows, its concentration of CO2 decreases and thus other components find more opportunity to diffuse through the membrane. As such, the permeate concentration of CO2 steadily drops from its initial value of 84.97% to 82.44% at the outlet of the membrane. texts.orig: Fig. 10 illustrates the CO2 concentration (molar fraction) in feed/retentate flow for cocurrent and countercurrent flows. According to the simulation results, at the specified operation condition, the membrane can reduce the CO2 concentration from feed's original value of 20.0% down to about 12.76% for both concurrent and countercurrent flows. This implies that for further retentate purity, implementation of higher membrane area or higher pressure is required. In the following section we show results of different sensitivity analyses that study the impact of different variables on membrane performance.

texts.text: Membrane separation, as discussed earlier, according to Eq. (5), has direct functionality to permeance, pressure and area. There are some indirect parameters that dramatically affect the decision on membrane application such as selectivity and/or feed concentration. In this section we study the impact of these parameters on membrane performance.

texts.orig: 6.1. Impact of feed pressure and membrane area
texts.text: 6.1. Impact of feed pressure and membrane area

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Feed pressure and membrane area are two very critical design parameters. High feed pressure translates to higher OPEX whilst high membrane area is linked to high CAPEX. Therefore, it is always desired to have both these values as low as possible. But any change in these parameters affects the permeate and retentate composition as well as flowrate and pressure. We study here the impact of these two parameters on other variables.

texts.orig: One of the important variables which is affected by membrane area or feed pressure is permeate flowrate that is traditionally addressed as stage-cut ( /DC2 = Fp / Ff 0 ). Fig. 11 shows simulation results for the combined effect of membrane area and pressure on /DC2 . When feed pressure is increased, permeate flowrate and thus stage-cut also increases. This can be explained as a result of increasing driving force (Eq. (5)) which steadily increases mass transfer over membrane. Membrane area also has similar impact on permeate flowrate. Increasing both feed pressure and membrane area amplifies permeate flowrate further. For instance, whilst at membrane area of 4 m 2 /(mol/s) and pressure of 6 × 10 5 Pa, the stage-cut is 0.59%, this value increases to 50% at area of 55 m 2 /(mol/s) and pressure of 45 × 10 5 Pa.

texts.orig: It is noticed from the permeate profile that at any membrane area, with increase of feed pressure, permeate purity reaches a maximum after which the purity starts to decline with further pressure increase. It is also observed that at high membrane areas, the TC purity decline at high feed pressures is more significant than that at low membrane areas. For instance, at membrane area of 6 m 2 /(mol/s) when the feed pressure is 3 bar, the CO2 purity will be 91.73%. With increase of feed pressure to 12 bar, the purity will be 96.6% after which it insignificantly increase to a maximum value of 97.16% at 43 bar whilst with further increase of feed pressure, the purity will decrease. However, at higher areas such as 45 m 2 /(mol/s) the purity will reach a maximum value of 95.86% at 12 bar after which it will notably drop to 92.80% at 43 bar.

texts.text: As shown, the behaviour of permeate purity, having clear optimum point, is an interesting and insightful outcome. The figure, itself, proposes optimal feed gas compression pressure which is an invaluable outcome for optimised membrane process design. However, a thorough discussion in Fig. 13 could be enriched by considering recovery (Fig. 14) as well. Recovery is defined as proportion of TC in the outlet permeate against the inlet feed:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Rec = ypout ypout xi int Ff int (27)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recalling Eq. (5), the key three mass transfer influential parameters are permeance, membrane area and feed pressure (driving force). Having permeance at constant value, it is expected that mass transfer and thus recovery are elevated with increase of feed pressure and/or membrane area. In agreement with this expectation, Fig. 14 depicts that at membrane area of 4 m 2 /(mol/s) and pressure of 6 bar, the recovery is only 1.17% (stage-cut: 0.59% 2 ). In such a condition, permeate will have CO2 purity of 95.54% whilst the value for retentate will be 48.22%, that is, only 0.58% less than the original CO2 fraction of 48.5% in the feed gas. With increasing feed gas pressure (and thus driving force) and/or membrane area the rate of mass transfer over the membrane increases (Fig. 11) resulting in higher recovery (Fig. 14). As a result, more CO2 penetrate through the membrane and CO2 concentration of retentate, therefore, decreases (Fig. 13b). Moreover at higher area and/or feed pressure, other less-permeable components, also, find chance to penetrate through the membrane. This results in CO2 purity reduction. For instance, at feed pressure of 42 bar and membrane area of 45 m 2 /(mol/s), the fraction of CO2 in retentate will be 10.10% that is about 79.2% less than original feed gas. This translates into a CO2 recovery value of 88.82%. However, such a recovery is to the cost of CO2 purity reduction down to 92.95%.

Having permeance at constant value, it is expected that mass transfer and thus recovery are elevated with increase of feed pressure and/or membrane area. In agreement with this expectation, Fig. 14 depicts that at membrane area of 4 m 2 /(mol/s) and pressure of 6 bar, the recovery is only 1.17% (stage-cut: 0.59% 2 ). In such a condition, permeate will have CO2 purity of 95.54% whilst the value for retentate will be 48.22%, that is, only 0.58% less than the original CO2 fraction of 48.5% in the feed gas. With increasing feed gas pressure (and thus driving force) and/or membrane area the rate of mass transfer over the membrane increases (Fig. 11) resulting in higher recovery (Fig. 14). As a result, more CO2 penetrate through the membrane and CO2 concentration of retentate, therefore, decreases (Fig. 13b). Moreover at higher area and/or feed pressure, other less-permeable components, also, find chance to penetrate through the membrane. This results in CO2 purity reduction. For instance, at feed pressure of 42 bar and membrane area of 45 m 2 /(mol/s), the fraction of CO2 in retentate will be 10.10% that is about 79.2% less than original feed gas. This translates into a CO2 recovery value of 88.82%. However, such a recovery is to the cost of CO2 purity reduction down to 92.95%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Therefore, this trade-off should be carefully tailored in membrane system design. High feed pressure and/or high membrane area, increases TC recovery and reduces its concentration in retentate which is desirable. whilst on the other hand, membrane area has negative impact on the purity of TC in permeate which most of the times is not desirable. Therefore, it is critical for a given gas mixture, to choose right feed pressure in combination with right membrane area. This choice of both feed pressure and membrane area should be done in tandem.

High feed pressure and/or high membrane area, increases TC recovery and reduces its concentration in retentate which is desirable. whilst on the other hand, membrane area has negative impact on the purity of TC in permeate which most of the times is not desirable. Therefore, it is critical for a given gas mixture, to choose right feed pressure in combination with right membrane area. This choice of both feed pressure and membrane area should be done in tandem.

This can be attributed to the reduction of mass transfer driving force by increasing the permeate pressure and thus decreasing the differential pressure ( xi Pf -yi Pp ). The interesting observation from Fig. 16a is the optimality region for permeate purity. It is noticed that for the given problem, the maximum purity lies at lower feed pressures and also lower permeate discharge pressures. For quantitative investigation we have chosen three different scenarios but all with Pf /Ppout ratio of 30. These are ( Pf , Ppout ) of (15 bar, 0.5 bar), (30 bar, 1.0 bar) and (45 bar, 1.5 bar). For first scenario of (15 bar, 0.5 bar) the purity is 85.1% with recovery of 21.4%. With the second scenario, the purity slightly declines to 84.9% whilst recovery becomes more than double, 43.9%. For the last scenario (45 bar, 1.5 bar), the purity is 83.5% with recovery of 60.9%. It could be concluded that for the cases when only the permeate purity is important and the feed is not naturally high-pressure, the economical option might be compressing feed to lower pressures and compensate the driving force by arranging vacuum condition at permeate discharge section. However, when high stage-cut and recovery are also required, then feed pressure will be the definitive parameter.

texts.orig: In summary, the interesting observation is that permeance shows more impact on membrane performance than ideal selectivity. Production of high-purity permeate happens at low permeances. Conversely, high permeance results in high flux (stage-cut) at low selectivities. High permeance does not show significant impact on stage-cut at high selectivities. Furthermore, stage-cut is not very sensitive to selectivity at low permeances, but it notably decreases at high permeances when selectivity is increased. As per the usual discussion from the membrane synthesis point of view, manufacturing high selectivity membranes is difficult and since low selectivity membranes can satisfy similar recovery objective, we can conclude that except the cases when specifically either high flux (requiring high permeance) or high purity (requiring high selectivity) is targeted, in all other cases membranes with medium selectivity and medium permeance are sufficiently good choices for gas separation resulting in efficient TC recovery (Fig. 15c). This result informed by validated modelling and simulation, criticises general consensus with stringent target to manufacture high selectivity membranes. It is advisable to focus more on producing membranes with good permeances whilst having acceptable selectivity. At this point we reflect on the discussions in Section 1 emphasising that optimal material designs are a strong function of systems design.

texts.orig: One of the important questions that a membrane manufacturer may encounter is the length of the membrane module. We know that A = 2 /EM RoLN . Having area ( A ) and fibre diameter ( Ro ) constant, we should have a constant value for LN , i.e. the multiplication of length ( L ) by number of fibre ( N ). Now, a design question arises; given a membrane with specific area A to be manufactured into one module, which of the following two choices are preferred? (1) less number of fibres but with longer membrane, or (2) more fibres but shorter membrane. We have curiously studied this problem for countercurrent flow with the results shown in Fig. 18. The figure shows the stage-cut, CO2 fraction in permeate and retentate as well as the overall CO2 recovery.

texts.orig: It is interestingly noticed that, for the given condition, the permeate purity, stage-cut and recovery decrease with increase of membrane length. For example, at membrane length of 0.5 m the permeate purity is 0.88. However, at membrane length of 2.5 m the value becomes 0.79. The same trend is depicted for recovery and stage-cut. This means that under the given condition for a membrane with fixed area of A it is more advantageous to have shorter length resulting in higher number of fibres. This may be reasoned that shorter length helps purity of CO2 remains higher, and when designed with higher number of fibres, the stage-cut is taken care of, resulting in higher recovery. It should, however, be noted that the decision on what length to choose is related to manufacturing limitations, as lengths below certain limits might not be practical. The cost of module is another consideration in such decisions. The immediate estimation is that small-length strategy will increase the total number of installed modules

texts.orig: 1. When the concentration of TC in the inlet feed is high, membranes can perform very well compared to other kinds of separation systems, resulting in high purity permeates (Fig. 20a). However, they fail in satisfying retentate with low TC concentration (Fig. 20d). As an example, we again introduce the discussion of natural gas sweetening. Traditionally, the sweetening was carried out with amine-based absorption/desorption processes. In 1980s, application of membrane to this process was introduced for the cases where the sour gas concentration is high. It was soon realised that though membrane is very economical to separate bulk of sour gas, it was not capable of reaching effluent natural gas (retentate) with sour gas concentration less than 2% (enforced by pipeline specifications). Therefore, a hybrid of membrane and amine system was introduced, so that the membrane takes the responsibility of bulk separation and amine-system further reduces the sour concentration of retentate (Koros and Fleming, 1993; Kohl and Nielsen, 1997).

texts.orig: Most of the cases, the concentration of TC in feed gas is notably low. As shown in Fig. 20, a single-stage membrane cannot produce high quality permeate or retentate. One may argue that selection of proper feed gas pressure and/or membrane area might help reaching satisfactory results. Fig. 21 illustrates the TC concentration of permeate for a feed gas with 10% TC over a wide ranges of feed pressure and membrane area. As seen in this figure, the maximum permeate purity is attained at low membrane areas and high pressures. For example, the maximum purity shown in Fig. 21 is 0.67.6% under the given condition. Even, such operating condition will not be desirable as the low membrane area results in significantly low stage-cut and thus low recovery. At the mentioned condition stage-cut will be only 1.38% and the overall recovery will be just 9.3%. Therefore, the optimal design that could satisfy both recovery and purity will be at higher membrane areas and appropriate pressure that will definitely result in TC purity much less than the above value.

texts.orig: The solution has been a combination of a few membrane stages, in parallel or series to reach higher qualities. Such arrangements however, result in higher CAPEX (due to high membrane areas) and OPEX (due to high compression costs) making the appropriateness of membranes debatable for such conditions of low concentration feeds. In such scenarios, membranes may not be the best available technology (BAT) and other separation technologies might be more competitive. Selection of the right option will require detailed technoeconomical investigations. The success of membrane system will be very much dependent on process synthesis, configuration and design. Enumerating the potential of membranes in such low TC feed scenarios can only be achieved through MSE activities.

texts.orig: The main objective of our study has been to develop a methodology for optimal configuration of multi-stage membrane system satisfying both minimum CAPEX and OPEX objectives. This requires at least two important issues; firstly, an efficient numerical model capable of handling multicomponent flows and pressure profiles over hollow fibre membrane is necessary. Secondly, an insight over influential variables and their interactive role in overall design are vital.

texts.orig: We presented a computation algorithm for rigorous solution of hollow fibre membrane systems with multicomponent feed gas. The computation method allowed systematic analysis of the impact of different parameters on overall performance of the membrane system. The case study on natural gas sweetening resulted in better insights and understanding that would direct our membrane systems design.

texts.orig: It was, moreover, highlighted that high selectivity does not necessarily have notable impact on membrane performance, rather, a medium selectivity (<60 in the given example) combined with medium permeance ( ∼ 300-500 × 10 -10 mol/s m 2 Pa in the given example) is more advantageous. The exact values of these parameters could be also obtained through techno-economic analysis containing both CAPEX and OPEX objectives. The results also quantitatively confirmed the general consensus that a single-stage membrane cannot satisfy a given separation objectives below a certain percentage of target components in feed gas. The algorithm which was presented, could be easily replicated by prospective modelers and it could be used for optimal design of multi-stage membrane system being the ultimate goal of our current study.
